summary: My great grandmother, Lillian Gamm, was born in 1908 in Elizabethgrad, Russia. She immigrated to the United States as a young child with her family, arriving at Ellis Island in 1910 after a difficult journey in steerage on the ship Furnessia. Despite financial struggles, Lillian valued education and eventually graduated from Julia Richmond High School. She worked as a bookkeeper in the Bronx and balanced family life with her career, even returning to work decades after her husband's passing. Her story reflects perseverance and the immigrant experience in early 20th century America.

Elizabethgrad was the birthplace of my great grandmother Lillian Gamm in 1908. It is located near the Inhul River in Russia, where her family lived before deciding to leave due to anti-Jewish pogroms. This place was part of the Pale of Settlement, an area where many Jewish families faced persecution. Elizabethgrad represents the difficult origins from which my family started before immigrating to the United States.

The Inhul River is near Elizabethgrad, where my great grandmother Lillian Gamm was born in 1908. It was part of the region in Russia where her family lived before emigrating to America. The river marks the geographic area tied to her early life and the difficult conditions Jewish families faced in that region. It’s a small but meaningful detail in the story of her journey from Russia to the United States.

The United States was the destination for my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family when they fled Russia in 1910. They arrived through Ellis Island after a long and uncomfortable journey, seeking freedom from anti-Jewish violence in the Pale of Settlement. America was a place of opportunity and challenge for them, where Lillian worked hard, pursued education, and eventually built a life in Manhattan and the Bronx. The country gave her a chance to live freely and follow her dreams despite the hardships of immigration.

Esther was my great grandmother Lillian Gamm’s older sister, who was four years old when they left Elizabethgrad for America. She traveled with Lillian and their mother Pascha on the ship Furnessia, arriving at Ellis Island in 1910. Esther was part of the family’s early immigrant experience, sharing the challenges of adjusting to life in the United States. Though details about her later life aren’t as clear, she was an important part of Lillian’s childhood and migration story.

Pascha was the mother of my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her sister Esther. She made the difficult decision to leave Elizabethgrad with her children to escape the pogroms in Russia’s Pale of Settlement. In 1910, Pascha traveled with her family on the ship Furnessia from Odessa to Glasgow and then to Ellis Island. She was the matriarch who guided the family through the challenges of immigration and resettlement in America.

Odessa was the port city from which my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family departed Russia in 1910. They likely took a ship from Odessa to Glasgow as part of their journey to the United States. Odessa was a key point in their migration route, representing the last stop in their homeland before crossing the Atlantic. It was part of the Pale of Settlement region where Jewish families like mine faced persecution.

Glasgow, Scotland was a stopover for my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family on their way to America in 1910. After leaving Odessa, they traveled to Glasgow before boarding the ship Furnessia to Ellis Island. This city was a brief but important part of their journey, marking their passage from Eastern Europe to the United States. It was a waypoint on the long and difficult trip my family took to seek a better life.

Ellis Island was the entry point to America for my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family on April 19th, 1910. After traveling in the crowded and uncomfortable 4th steerage on the ship Furnessia, they arrived at Ellis Island where they went through Customs. This place was the gateway to their new life in the United States, where they faced the challenges of adapting to a new culture and language. Ellis Island represents the beginning of their American journey.

Furnessia was the ship that carried my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family from Glasgow to Ellis Island in 1910. They traveled in the 4th steerage, which was the cheapest and most crowded part of the ship, reserved for the poorest passengers. The journey was long and uncomfortable, but it was their passage to the United States and a new life away from persecution in Russia. Furnessia is a symbol of the hardships endured during immigration.

The Pale of Settlement was the region in Russia and Ukraine where my great grandmother Lillian Gamm’s family lived before emigrating. It was a place marked by anti-Jewish pogroms that made life dangerous and difficult for Jewish families like mine. The violence and persecution in the Pale of Settlement were the main reasons my family decided to leave for the United States in 1910. This area represents the harsh conditions they fled to find freedom and safety.

Manhattan was the first place my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family lived after arriving in America in 1910. They stayed at 57 East 98th Street while adjusting to life in a new country where they only spoke Yiddish. Although they moved to New Haven, Connecticut for a time to live with relatives, they returned to Manhattan once they felt more comfortable. Manhattan was the starting point of their American experience before eventually moving to the Bronx.

New Haven, Connecticut was where my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family moved temporarily after first settling in Manhattan. They lived with relatives there as they adjusted to life in America and gained confidence in their new environment. After some time in New Haven, they moved back to Manhattan to live independently. This move was part of their gradual adaptation to American life during the early 20th century.

Yiddish was the only language my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family spoke when they arrived in America in 1910. This made adjusting to life in Manhattan and later New Haven challenging as they had to learn English and navigate a new culture. Despite the language barrier, Lillian was dedicated to her education and eventually succeeded academically. Yiddish connects to their heritage and the immigrant experience of maintaining cultural identity while adapting to a new country.

Julia Richmond High School was where my great grandmother Lillian Gamm graduated after convincing her parents to let her return to school. Although she had to drop out earlier to help support her family financially, Lillian’s love of learning motivated her to finish her education there. Graduating from Julia Richmond High School was a significant achievement for her as an immigrant woman in early 20th century America. It helped pave the way for her career as a bookkeeper in the Bronx.

The Bronx was where my great grandmother Lillian Gamm and her family eventually settled after living in Manhattan and New Haven. She worked there as a bookkeeper because she was skilled with numbers. It was also where she met her future husband at a Halloween party and later raised their family. The Bronx represents the place where Lillian built her adult life, balancing work and family as an immigrant woman in America.
